transcript.transcript: Hello, my name is Kimberly Rice Brown. I'm a member of the Post Falls Historical Society and chairman of their oral history program, and we are very happy today to be interviewing Louis M Brown. I am at his home, and I will be talking to him about his family and his personal relationships in Idaho. This oral history is conducted with the support of the Idaho Historical Society as part of America 250 in Idaho. This project is an interview, and it supports the commemorations mission to advance the ideals of the American Revolution, which include equality, liberty, and justice through meaningful community engagement, and it recognizes, appreciates, and commemorates Idaho's journey in America's legacy. Lewis and Brown, first, let me thank you so much for your time today. This interview will allow you to reflect and share your memories and experiences about local history. Through these questions, I hope to gain insights into your personal and family ties to Idaho. I will begin the interview with some general questions to start the conversation, and we can get into more specific topics from there. During the interview, I may adjust the order of some of the questions to allow for a natural flow of topics, and I might ask unscripted follow up questions to clarify your answers. So before we jump into the questions, can I have you state and spell your name? Lewis Brown l e w I s m Brown b r o w n and loo. Uh, with regard to your name, can you give me a little insight as to how your mother chose that name and how it ties into Idaho history? Well, it ties into Idaho history insofar as the earliest explorers, Lewis and Clark, one of them was named Lewis, of course, and she happened to be named Lois, but I think she had in mind as she was trying to pull a little trick. And because people always referred to her as Lewis, and it sounded enough like Lewis to warrant her giving it that name, and it was kind of a double double whammy, I guess. Well, as as you lived in the Lolo, Montana area and the Fish Creek area. The Lewis and Clark Expedition went right through that particular area of Montana and over into Idaho. So if you have any insight into the Lewis and Clark experience, do you have any of those experiences that you might be able to reflect on. The Lewis and Clark experience? Yes. The Lewis and Clark Highway, they of course, they came right up the Lolo Creek over the top of the pass and then stayed on course all the way through the the state. What's now the state line? Uh, trail. And have you traveled any of the state line trail down to Lewiston? I have. I've done it on the trail and the road, of course, that goes along the state line and drops down down by Weippie, I but that, of course, you know, is not the exact route of the original trail. Well, I want to ask you about your family's connection to that part of the Bitterroot Mountains. Uh, the Idaho side, which your family was involved in trapping, logging, and mining. So are you familiar with any of those early trappers that lived in Lochsa the area? The earliest trappers that I know of were a couple of them, uh, Fred Schott, who was a he was an old man. And. Still living on our homestead down on Fish Creek in Montana. And, he was he caretaker at the property at that time, and he talked about some of the adventures that he had in the in the lochsa trapping. The other person that was influential then was my great granddad. Um, Burt Wendover, who was he married my grandmother in 1922. And, uh, Burt was he was a trapper in that lochsa country. And he was also involved in, uh, the homestead that on on Fish Creek on the Montana side of the line. Well, as far as those two men, how did their how are their names commemorated in Idaho? Well, uh, Wendover Campground and Wendover Ridge were two features in the lochsa, just a little ways from Powell. And as far as Fred Short. What's his name? Didn't quite make it onto the map. He was like, according to Bud Moore, he said he put, uh, he went naming Short Creek. Uh, just that short creek. And it turns out it's Short Creek and that's up near, um, near the Powell area. Now, as far as the Powell area, your your parents were involved with an early settler there, Andrew Erickson. Can you tell us a little bit about Andrew Erickson? Andrew Erickson Well, of course, he was the person that started what is known as the Foxtail Lodge. And as I understand it, he built that in about 19. 30 and the only show in town at that time as far as that upper Loch shore country. Lodge. And. Andrew. Besides being a trapper, he ran some, uh, dude services out of. Rock star Lodge. During that period. Off and on. Um, my. Father was, uh, he, uh. He my dad worked for him seasonally. Got to know him pretty well. And my mother also worked there one summer as a cook at the What's the weather been in there? Probably in the mid to early 30s. So your ties to the Lochsa Lodge kind of your early. Your parents were pretty involved with Andrew Erickson. Did did they, uh, keep up in touch with him after he sold the Lochsa Lodge? Well, Andrew and his wife moved to Salem, Oregon, after selling the lodge and exactly when that was. But, um, I remember they they did stop by. And they sold out, but I can't remember when that was. Well, you had some experience also, uh, your father did with the C6 and the Forest Service. They have an operation today at Powell and a very long time operation in the lochsa. Can you tell us anything about your dad's involvement with the C.C.C. crews? Well. As I say, he was he was a hire hacker and worked for the Forest Service as well. And it was seasonal and his job became available. So he did packing for both as well as the Luxor Lodge when packing wasn't otherwise employed. Well, you as a young kid then, uh, when was your what is your first memory of the Lochsa area? And did you visit what is now the Bitterroot. Selway Wilderness? Did you visit that as a kid before it was designated as a wilderness? Yeah, it was about the first time we were in there was about, I would say close to 1958. It was on the same dirt road that goes in to the into Elk Summit. And we went in with my dad and I. I might have been, oh, probably 12 or so years old or approximately. Um, we went in from hike from Elk Summit into Maple Lake, and all we had to use for camping equipment was from army surplus packsack type stuff and heavy sleeping bags that weighed 5 or £6. Maybe some of them were a pork and the others were probably heavy. Cotton or early Dacron type sleeping bags and. Other than that, we just had a couple of cans of food and their coffee, and I wasn't old enough to pack much of a load, so I didn't probably carry as much as the others, that's for sure. My shoes were a pair of new heeled boots types that we got just for the trip and. they were. They were not what I should have had on my feet at the time, because I had blisters from one end to the other because of. So yeah, but it was quite an it was an adventure. It was enjoyable. And our purpose, of course, was we were looking for, um, lost gold. Are we looking for lost gold or buried treasures? And, uh, of course, we didn't find anything. But it was an adventure that was interesting for us. For me, it was a tremendous experience because that was probably a ten mile hike, at least from each each direction. The impact of, I guess, the wilderness. Then you had that from a very early age, but you have continued with that long time hiking and appreciation of the wilderness for till present day. Is that right? The last time I was into Elk Summit was this past fall of about in September. Went up and hiked with my wife up to Friday's pass. And it was. It was hot. Yeah, we still made it into the box really every year for the past 50 years. Well, the obvious changes, I suppose, to the land include forest fires and trail systems. So you want to share a little bit on what happened and how it has changed the access to a lot of that wilderness area? Well, yes. Since in recent times where we've had quite a few wildfires in in that area and other wilderness areas as well. And yes, there are recent fires and smoke coming pretty heavy over the hill from the Moose Creek drains. So and of course, logging, it also had its impacts. A lot of that logging. Logging is. Probably in the 30 to 50 year old range. And sales in the upper end of the lock locks are fixed. Now, as I understand it, you not only hiked as a kid, but you also did horse camping and have access. The wilderness, I suppose. Even even by plane on occasion. You want to share any of those memories? Well, um. Further, of course. Most recent is we flew in just a few years ago from Moscow in a little private charter into the Moose Creek Ranger Station. Moose Creek airport and, uh, on the lower end, and, uh, hiked out by about two weeks out. And it was quite enjoyable The horses we've gone in. Up to about 15 years ago. Gone in at least once every year. And that was using my cousin's horses, always looking for gold, always looking for gold. We did have some real. But most of the area is clear down to, um. Yeah, black. Uh. People. Heather and Don from Elk Summit. Well, those covered kind of large areas, probably from the Bitterroot Mountains, say Blodgett Pass all the way down to the Selway area. So that area covers a huge amount of country. If we get back to the ideas of equality, some of the things that are so important to everything, our founding fathers were working off on that idea of the equality of of, uh, experience, you might say, um, the land being open and free for Americans to enjoy. Uh, you got into the land management business. If you thought of a way of, say, the legacy of public lands to the public, what do you think the greatest legacy of public lands are to the general public. Well, there are a lot of a lot of things that benefited the public benefits from public. Of course, you have the areas that are developed and that kind of. Bitterroot, Selway, wilderness that are all they are with designated wilderness areas. And despite the fact that, uh, access can be hard sometimes in those areas, the wilderness values are important, very important, even where you can see natural areas working, um, whether it be wildfire or the. Encroachment of small timber into an area, it really doesn't matter. Wilderness has a lot of value just for the sake of wilderness. Values such as air and water. Carbon capture, all that stuff. In the time period that you interacted with the Bitterroot. Selway. Have you seen any changes in game and fish populations? Well, I well, I can't say for sure. The biggest time of reports of wildlife after the period, after some of those fires has been pretty good to increase. But, uh, I don't have a lot to say about that. Real hard, fast data. If you had an area in the Lochsa that was maybe your favorite areas that you have either visited or backpacked to. Uh, do you have any favorites that you would like to revisit? Well, there are a couple of lakes that. That I haven't been into yet, that's for sure. I would like to revisit those. The other, uh, there are a lot of lakes that are really, uh, really nice, but I don't have the legs to go up into them anymore. So. Now, do you know anything about any Native American trails that were in the Luxor that you heard any stories about? Well, stories I did hear. That horse, the Lolo Divide Trail, was very. It was the main travel route. Lewis and Clark. Back there. And as well as, of course, Native Americans for many, many years before Lewis and Clark. And what they talked about was, um, there was one place in particular that where they crossed the river. Somewhere near, uh, Powell and climbed up the opposite side over, um, Goat Roost, down into the Wind Lakes area and up over Friday Pass. That's from information, I was told. Yes. Now, we know that a written public publication on the Luxor story has been compiled by Bud Moore of the United States Forest Service. Did you and your family ever have any interaction with Bud Moore? Yes. Um, parents knew Bud Moore, both of them. And I've. I've been around visiting our place. He did stop one time. Take his great granddad, Bert Wendover, down the Luxor highway. So yeah, they knew my my dad and mother. Um, but of course, was just written. The, uh, he has written the major edition of history of the block, sort of the whole country. So, yeah, this book is is really very, very well done. A lot of time on that. Is there anything, Lou, that you wanted to share with regard to that part of Idaho or anything in the Post Falls area that stands out that you would like to share in this interview? Well, of course, we've lived over in the Post Falls area for, around 40 years. So we've been in this area quite a long time. Enjoyed the Post Falls Pauline area hike quite a lot in northern Idaho as well. That's all I have to say. It's a fast growing area right now. Of course, it has a lot of a lot of amenities and a lot of area that we're moving here, but growth is happening quickly. Well, Lew, I would like to thank you today for this oral video history. And we hope to continue chatting as your as you would like to share more stories. So thank you for your time today and thank you for preserving and sharing more of Idaho history. Thank you.

lines.line: # Introduction to the Oral History Interview

> The speaker introduces herself as Kimberly Rice Brown, a member of the Post Falls Historical Society and chairman of their oral history program. She is conducting an interview with Louis M Brown at his home, supported by the Idaho Historical Society as part of America 250 in Idaho. The project aims to reflect on personal and family ties to Idaho and to commemorate the ideals of the American Revolution through community engagement.

metadata.title: Introduction to the Oral History Interview
metadata.synopsis: The speaker introduces herself as Kimberly Rice Brown, a member of the Post Falls Historical Society and chairman of their oral history program. She is conducting an interview with Louis M Brown at his home, supported by the Idaho Historical Society as part of America 250 in Idaho. The project aims to reflect on personal and family ties to Idaho and to commemorate the ideals of the American Revolution through community engagement.

lines.line: # Significance of the Interviewee's Name

> The speaker asks the interviewee about the origin of his name, Louis, and its connection to Idaho history. The interviewee explains that his name is linked to the explorer Lewis from the Lewis and Clark Expedition, and shares a humorous anecdote about his mother's naming choice, which reflects a historical and personal significance.

metadata.title: Significance of the Interviewee's Name
metadata.synopsis: The speaker asks the interviewee about the origin of his name, Louis, and its connection to Idaho history. The interviewee explains that his name is linked to the explorer Lewis from the Lewis and Clark Expedition, and shares a humorous anecdote about his mother's naming choice, which reflects a historical and personal significance.

lines.line: # Family History in the Bitterroot Mountains

> The interviewee discusses his family's historical connection to the Bitterroot Mountains, focusing on early trappers and their activities in the area. He mentions specific ancestors who were involved in trapping, logging, and mining, and how their names have been commemorated in the region.

metadata.title: Family History in the Bitterroot Mountains
metadata.synopsis: The interviewee discusses his family's historical connection to the Bitterroot Mountains, focusing on early trappers and their activities in the area. He mentions specific ancestors who were involved in trapping, logging, and mining, and how their names have been commemorated in the region.

lines.line: # Early Settlers and Employment History

> The conversation shifts to the Powell area where the interviewee's parents were involved with an early settler, Andrew Erickson, who started the Foxtail Lodge. The interviewee shares details about his parents' employment with Erickson and their personal connection to him, highlighting the social and economic interactions of the time.

metadata.title: Early Settlers and Employment History
metadata.synopsis: The conversation shifts to the Powell area where the interviewee's parents were involved with an early settler, Andrew Erickson, who started the Foxtail Lodge. The interviewee shares details about his parents' employment with Erickson and their personal connection to him, highlighting the social and economic interactions of the time.

lines.line: # Personal Wilderness Experiences

> The interviewee recounts personal and family experiences related to wilderness exploration, including his father's involvement with the C6 and the Forest Service. He shares vivid memories of hiking and camping in the wilderness, emphasizing the adventurous and challenging nature of these experiences.

metadata.title: Personal Wilderness Experiences
metadata.synopsis: The interviewee recounts personal and family experiences related to wilderness exploration, including his father's involvement with the C6 and the Forest Service. He shares vivid memories of hiking and camping in the wilderness, emphasizing the adventurous and challenging nature of these experiences.

lines.line: # The Legacy of Public Lands

> The discussion turns to the broader impact of wilderness and public lands on society. The interviewee reflects on the environmental and recreational values of public lands, discussing changes in wildlife, forest fires, and trail systems. He emphasizes the importance of preserving these lands for their natural beauty and ecological benefits.

metadata.title: The Legacy of Public Lands
metadata.synopsis: The discussion turns to the broader impact of wilderness and public lands on society. The interviewee reflects on the environmental and recreational values of public lands, discussing changes in wildlife, forest fires, and trail systems. He emphasizes the importance of preserving these lands for their natural beauty and ecological benefits.

lines.line: # Reflections on Local Growth and History

> In the concluding part of the interview, the interviewee reflects on his life and the changes in the Post Falls area, where he has lived for about 40 years. He comments on the rapid growth and development of the area, highlighting both the challenges and opportunities it presents.

metadata.title: Reflections on Local Growth and History
metadata.synopsis: In the concluding part of the interview, the interviewee reflects on his life and the changes in the Post Falls area, where he has lived for about 40 years. He comments on the rapid growth and development of the area, highlighting both the challenges and opportunities it presents.

lines.line: # Introduction to the Interview

> The speaker introduces herself as Kimberly Rice Brown, a member of the Post Falls Historical Society, and outlines the purpose of the interview with Louis M Brown. The interview is part of a project supported by the Idaho Historical Society to commemorate America's 250th anniversary, focusing on themes like liberty, equality, and justice. The speaker aims to explore Louis M Brown's personal and family history in Idaho.

metadata.title: Introduction to the Interview
metadata.synopsis: The speaker introduces herself as Kimberly Rice Brown, a member of the Post Falls Historical Society, and outlines the purpose of the interview with Louis M Brown. The interview is part of a project supported by the Idaho Historical Society to commemorate America's 250th anniversary, focusing on themes like liberty, equality, and justice. The speaker aims to explore Louis M Brown's personal and family history in Idaho.

lines.line: # Naming and Historical Significance

> The speaker asks Louis about the historical significance of his name, which is connected to the explorers Lewis and Clark. Louis explains that his mother named him after these explorers, reflecting on how his name has been a point of both personal and historical significance, linking him to Idaho's early exploration history.

metadata.title: Naming and Historical Significance
metadata.synopsis: The speaker asks Louis about the historical significance of his name, which is connected to the explorers Lewis and Clark. Louis explains that his mother named him after these explorers, reflecting on how his name has been a point of both personal and historical significance, linking him to Idaho's early exploration history.

lines.line: # Family History and Early Trappers

> Louis discusses his family's historical connection to the Bitterroot Mountains, focusing on early trappers and family members who were involved in trapping, logging, and mining. He mentions specific ancestors and their roles in the area, highlighting how these activities shaped his family's history and connection to Idaho.

metadata.title: Family History and Early Trappers
metadata.synopsis: Louis discusses his family's historical connection to the Bitterroot Mountains, focusing on early trappers and family members who were involved in trapping, logging, and mining. He mentions specific ancestors and their roles in the area, highlighting how these activities shaped his family's history and connection to Idaho.

lines.line: # Early Settlers and Employment

> The speaker inquires about Louis's parents' involvement with Andrew Erickson, an early settler who established the Foxtail Lodge. Louis shares how his parents worked for Erickson, providing insights into the early economic activities and community interactions in the Lochsa area of Idaho.

metadata.title: Early Settlers and Employment
metadata.synopsis: The speaker inquires about Louis's parents' involvement with Andrew Erickson, an early settler who established the Foxtail Lodge. Louis shares how his parents worked for Erickson, providing insights into the early economic activities and community interactions in the Lochsa area of Idaho.

lines.line: # Personal Wilderness Experiences

> Louis recounts his earliest memories of the Luxor area, describing his adventures and the rugged conditions during his initial wilderness explorations. He reflects on the challenges and the beauty of the wilderness, emphasizing the profound impact these experiences had on his appreciation for Idaho's natural landscapes.

metadata.title: Personal Wilderness Experiences
metadata.synopsis: Louis recounts his earliest memories of the Luxor area, describing his adventures and the rugged conditions during his initial wilderness explorations. He reflects on the challenges and the beauty of the wilderness, emphasizing the profound impact these experiences had on his appreciation for Idaho's natural landscapes.

lines.line: # Changes in Wilderness and Public Land Legacy

> Louis discusses the changes he has observed in the wilderness areas, including the impact of forest fires and logging. He reflects on the legacy of public lands, emphasizing their importance for environmental values such as air quality, water, and carbon capture, and how these lands offer recreational and natural benefits to the public.

metadata.title: Changes in Wilderness and Public Land Legacy
metadata.synopsis: Louis discusses the changes he has observed in the wilderness areas, including the impact of forest fires and logging. He reflects on the legacy of public lands, emphasizing their importance for environmental values such as air quality, water, and carbon capture, and how these lands offer recreational and natural benefits to the public.

lines.line: # Reflections on Idaho and Post Falls

> In concluding the interview, Louis reflects on his long-term residence in the Post Falls area, noting its rapid growth and the increasing number of amenities. He shares his continued connection and appreciation for the natural and community aspects of northern Idaho, highlighting the significant changes and developments in the region.

story.description: The interview discusses the personal and familial ties of Lewis M. Brown to Idaho, particularly focusing on historical connections and land usage in the Lochsa and Bitterroot regions. The conversation covers the legacy and impact of early settlers, including trappers and pioneers, on Idaho's history, and references to the Lewis and Clark expedition. It highlights Brown's family's involvement in logging, mining, and their connections with early settlers like Andrew Erickson. The discussion also touches on the changes wrought by forest fires, logging, and the management of wilderness areas, emphasizing the importance of preserving public lands for future generations. The interview concludes with appreciation for Idaho's rich history and Brown's lifetime connection to the land.
story.title: OH_Lewis M Brown_1.31.2025
